close_reason: Cache hit path now wraps bytes in Zeroizing before constructing SecretValue
description:
src/lib.rs:78: 'return Ok(SecretValue::new(entry.bytes.to_vec()))'.

The chain on the cache-hit path:
  entry.bytes: Zeroizing<Vec<u8>>
  entry.bytes.to_vec(): Vec<u8>          ← NOT zeroizing
  SecretValue::new(Vec<u8>):              ← wraps in Zeroizing inside

There is a temporary Vec<u8> on the stack between to_vec() and SecretValue::new() that is not Zeroizing. If the function returns before the wrap (it doesn't here, but a future refactor could insert intermediate code), the temporary Vec's allocation is freed without zeroing. Even in the current straight-line code, the temporary is moved into SecretValue::new, which itself does a re-wrap rather than transferring ownership of an already-Zeroizing buffer — so the Vec's heap allocation is reused but the wrapping changes; this is fine for the bytes-staying-allocated case, but the pattern invites future bugs.

This matters because the whole point of caching SecretValue bytes in Zeroizing is to ensure that drops zero the memory; doing copy-out-and-rewrap on every cache hit defeats that for the duration of the temporary, and any future mid-function early return makes it concrete.

Fix options, in order of preference:
1. Add SecretValue::from_zeroizing(Zeroizing<Vec<u8>>) -> Self to secretx-core. Then write 'return Ok(SecretValue::from_zeroizing(entry.bytes.clone()))'. Zeroizing<Vec<u8>> implements Clone, so the clone is itself zeroizing.
2. If touching secretx-core is out of scope, change CachedEntry to hold the bytes directly inside SecretValue and clone the SecretValue out... but SecretValue is not Clone (intentional, per zeroize discipline).
3. Document the brief non-zeroizing window and accept it.

Recommend option 1 — minimal API addition, zero ergonomic cost, eliminates a real (if narrow) zeroize gap.
title: Cache hit path leaks bytes through non-Zeroizing Vec on return
